title: Corrupt issue ref silently disappears from 'jjf ls' (no diagnostic)
body:
# Symptom

If a `refs/jjf/issues/<id>` ref gets pointed at something that
isn't a valid issue commit (corrupt fetch, accidental
`git update-ref`, etc.), `jjf ls` silently omits that issue
from its output with no warning. `jjf show <id>` reports
"issue not found in working copy" — indistinguishable from a
genuinely missing issue.

# Repro

```
$ jjf ls           # 2 issues
eed62d7  ...
34a30c5  ...
$ git update-ref refs/jjf/issues/eed62d7 \
    $(git hash-object -w --stdin <<<"junk")
$ jjf ls
34a30c5  ...   # eed62d7 silently gone, no warning
$ jjf show eed62d7
jjf: issue not found in working copy: eed62d7
```

# Risk profile

Across 10 projects this is unlikely to fire often, but when it
does it's the kind of failure that erodes trust ("where did my
ticket go?"). The data IS still in the repo (the ref still
exists, just doesn't parse) — recovery is possible if you can
diagnose.

# Fix candidate

A `jjf doctor` verb that:
- counts `refs/jjf/issues/*` and `refs/jjf/memories/*`,
- diffs against what the storage layer can parse,
- reports unreadable refs with their SHA,
- optionally suggests `git update-ref -d` to clear them.

Lighter alternative: `jjf ls` exits 0 but prints a final
stderr line `(1 ref unreadable; run jjf doctor)` when it
skips any. Cheap to add, immediately surfaces the problem.

# Confidence

Reproducible 100%. Tested in `scratchpad/qa/r07b-corrupt2/`.
